label: Female breast
quick_take: Breast cancer rose everywhere and in volume XII still rises almost everywhere — the cleanest universally-rising cancer of the twelve volumes. The most striking individual findings are the U-shapes in Japanese and Hawaii-Japanese women (where the fall around vol II–III was real, not artefact) and the world-record absolute rate in US-LA-County Filipino women (100.0).

findings.title: The biggest absolute rate in the whole breast dataset: LA-County Filipina women, vol X, ASR 100.0
findings.text: Still 83.2 in vol XII after some decline. This beats every US White and US Black subgroup.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — The biggest absolute rate in the whole breast dataset: LA-County Filipina women, vol X, ASR 100.0

findings.title: Clean U-shape in Japan and migrant Japanese women
findings.text: Miyagi Prefecture 15.3 (vol I) → 12.8 (vol II) → 74.6 (vol XII); Nagasaki 26.3 → 20.3 (vol V) → 56.4. Hawaii Filipina 25.0 (vol II) → 24.7 (vol III) → 85.9 (vol XII). All "U" because the first volume under-ascertained then the catch-up was steep.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Clean U-shape in Japan and migrant Japanese women

findings.title: Singapore Chinese +785 % and Algeria Sétif +772 %
findings.text: the catch-up is not limited to East-Asian migrants.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Singapore Chinese +785 % and Algeria Sétif +772 %

findings.title: Extreme bimodal age curves in Hawaii Japanese women
findings.text: Peak shifts to age band 17 (75–79) rather than the usual 13–14 (60–69). In vol II the peak was 147 per 100 000 at 75–79. This is highly unusual — most populations have a late-60s peak or earlier. Hawaii Japanese women have a *later* peak than anyone else.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Extreme bimodal age curves in Hawaii Japanese women

findings.title: India Karunagappally women: early peak at age 12 (55–59)
findings.text: Notable because most breast-cancer peaks sit 10 years older; the premenopausal / perimenopausal hump in South Asian women has been described before but CI5 shows it here clearly with a large case count.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — India Karunagappally women: early peak at age 12 (55–59)

findings.title: US ethnic contrast in vol V: 6.7× spread
findings.text: (White 93.9 vs Korean 14.1). By vol XII the gap has closed to roughly 2.5× — but Filipina women *overshot* the US White rate to reach the top of the distribution. That inversion deserves its own note.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — US ethnic contrast in vol V: 6.7× spread

findings.title: Uganda Kyadondo: 11.39 (vol I) → 32.93 (vol X) → 32.0 (vol XII)
findings.text: Breast cancer in East Africa tripled in 60 years — the African epidemic story.
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — Uganda Kyadondo: 11.39 (vol I) → 32.93 (vol X) → 32.0 (vol XII)

findings.text: Breast screening roll-outs in different decades mean that a rising curve during vol VIII–X often captures a prevalence pool being worked through, not pure underlying incidence.
Hawaii Japanese late-peak numbers are small-N at band 17; sanity-check with larger aggregated data before making a story of it.

findings.title: The LA-County Filipina rate (100.0) is the highest breast-cancer ASR in CI5
findings.text: it beats every US White registry. Worth asking: acculturation effect, reproductive patterns, or registry differences?
findings.image_alt: CI5 trend chart — The LA-County Filipina rate (100.0) is the highest breast-cancer ASR in CI5

findings.text: The Japanese / migrant-Japanese U-shape: compare Miyagi with Hawaii Japanese across volumes to show the genetics-vs-environment separation.
African rise: Uganda Kyadondo, Algeria Sétif, Israel Arabs all share the same trajectory (+500 % to +770 %). A coordinated "breast cancer goes continental" piece writes itself.
